content: Insomnia may involve difficulty falling asleep, repeated night-time awakening, early morning awakening, reduced sleep quality, or shortened total sleep time, and should be interpreted together with daytime distress or impaired function.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; no patient identifiers or site contact details.

content: The overall goals of insomnia management include improving sleep quality or effective sleep duration, preventing short-term insomnia from becoming chronic, reducing insomnia-related physical or mental-health burden, restoring daytime function, and minimizing adverse effects from interventions.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; no patient identifiers or site contact details.

content: Common intervention categories for insomnia include medication treatment, psychological treatment, physical treatment, and traditional Chinese medicine approaches; the appropriate option depends on clinical assessment.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; no patient identifiers or site contact details.

content: For chronic insomnia that persists after identifying and addressing precipitating factors, cognitive behavioral therapy for insomnia and medication treatment are commonly considered major treatment options.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; no patient identifiers or site contact details.

content: Sleep-hygiene education is important in insomnia care and should provide a foundation before or alongside other treatment approaches.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; no patient identifiers or site contact details.

content: During sleep-related consultation, clinicians may ask about difficulty falling asleep, night awakenings, early awakening, unusual movements or behaviors during sleep, severity, duration, triggers, relieving factors, sleep-wake rhythm, and prior treatment.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; administrative rows and contact information were excluded.

content: Sleep scales and sleep diaries can help clinicians evaluate sleep symptoms and support diagnostic assessment.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; administrative rows and contact information were excluded.

content: Depending on the clinical situation, diagnostic evaluation may include blood tests, imaging such as CT or MRI, polysomnography, actigraphy, or other assessments selected by the clinician.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; administrative rows and contact information were excluded.

content: Headache can have many causes, and assessment generally requires detailed history-taking, physical examination, and necessary auxiliary examinations considered together.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; no patient identifiers or site contact details.

content: Depending on the case, clinicians may selectively use blood tests, neuroimaging, head and neck vascular evaluation, ophthalmology or otolaryngology assessment, lumbar puncture, or other tests to clarify headache-related problems.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; no patient identifiers or site contact details.

content: Headache education should emphasize recognition of warning signs that may suggest secondary causes such as space-occupying lesions, vascular disease, infection, metabolic disorders, or systemic disease.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; no patient identifiers or site contact details.

content: Migraine may present as unilateral or bilateral pulsating moderate-to-severe headache, often accompanied by photophobia, phonophobia, nausea, or vomiting, and may worsen with activity.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; no patient identifiers or site contact details.

content: Cervicogenic headache often begins in the neck or occipital region and may spread to the frontal, temporal, or orbital region, commonly on the affected side.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; no patient identifiers or site contact details.

content: Cervicogenic headache may be dull, distending, or pulling in quality, is often deep and non-pulsating, and may worsen with neck movement, fatigue, or poor posture while improving with rest.
anonymization_note: Translated and generalized from the knowledge-base workbook; no patient identifiers or site contact details.
